module: Module 1 |Introduction to Sustainability Matters in SMEs
content: Throughout this course, you will be exposed to certain terminology, words and definitions that are broadly used across the world when businesses think about climate change and sustainability and how to bring this conversation into the mainstream business strategies that they would discuss about. So let me take you through some of these terminology. Many of which you will also find in the prescribed textbook of this course, media and Climate Change, making Sense of Press Narratives. So I want you to go through the timeline as well mentioned in this book and look at how over the period of time we have used words such as global warming and different types of words. And today we are using words such as climate mitigation, climate adaptation, and climate resilience, to show that businesses really care for the environment and want to show how they want to incorporate sustainability measures into every aspect of their business. So we'll start with a few definitions here. And I want you to think about these definitions because you may have definitely studied about these definitions in your school curriculum, in your pre-university curriculum, but you should now think how you are going to apply these definitions into your businesses and into the business idea that you propose, or if you're going to be working in a business house, how you must make sure that the discussion focuses around these terminology. So we'll start with the definition of climate mitigation. What is climate mitigation? Climate mitigation is a human intervention to reduce emissions or enhance the sinks of greenhouse gases. Now, we do know that industrialization over several decades by humankind, has caused rampant damage to our environment in the form of effluence being let out into the air, water, and soil, and this buildup over a period of time has brought us to where we are today in a serious situation. So the mitigation that we are talking about has already started. Many businesses and countries have pledged, and you will read about it again in the book about the Paris Agreement, the COP summits. So you have to get that perspective first on how did this all began and when did countries, organizations commit to a plan to make sure that they understood that a human intervention was necessary for mitigation efforts. From there starts your journey to understand what is sustainability and what measures you can take to incorporate sustainability into the SMEs that you will work in or operate in. This human intervention can be in the form of a water bottle that you keep on the desk, a stainless steel water bottle or a coffee mug that you keep at work, which you would reuse again and again after washing it. So this is a simple concept, but imagine if you go to do business with someone or talk about sustainability to with someone, and you're carrying a plastic water bottle with you, or you're drinking from a paper cup and flinging it away. It shows that you as a individual have not thought carefully about this aspect of climate mitigation, and it could also showcase the other person that you are really not convinced yourself about climate mitigation. So climate mitigation starts at home. It starts from an individual, and then you can take it across as an idea to different entities whom you will interact with. So in the context of sustainability measures for SMEs. Climate mitigation is when an SME thinks about ways in which they could reduce emissions, that is by saving energy, conserving water, recycling their waste, and also thinking about giving back greenhouse gases in the form of capturing it through carbon Sinks. So that would be through measures where you would grow more plants and more trees because you have studied the process of photosynthesis and what happens when carbon dioxide is trapped or when carbon dioxide is released, and how are planet and the entire ecosystem breeds in this process. So I'm not going to get into those details because you've already studied them in your elementary schools, in your middle school, in your high school, because every educational institution today is making sure that they green the curriculum, that they are bringing in these aspects into the curriculum. Because it is our duty as educators and as universities and educational institutions, it is our duty to make sure that you understand this aspect very, very clearly, because you, the young people are going to be out there at the forefront, bearing the brunt of climate change and climate extreme events. So it is our duty to equip you with the skill sets and the knowledge required to adapt to this change and to show resilience to this change. And this is a topic that I'm also very passionate about because I have in every aspect of my curriculum, brought in a lot of conversation about climate mitigation, climate adaptation, and climate resilience. And this is the third, or the fourth course in the last 2 or 3 years that I have kind of set forth to teach, design and teach because. I have made sure that wherever I go, I bring these conversations back into the mainstream because we need to think about sustainability at its core. So for the postgraduate students here at IIM Bangalore. I have an elective called CEO's Agenda towards Climate Action, which talks about how the CEO at a company should drive climate change talks and initiatives and that is when it percolates down into the system, into its very core. So all of you are budding entrepreneurs. You are going to become the CEOs of your organization. I therefore would want you to think about this carefully and lead from the front, these efforts towards climate mitigation, adaptation, and resilience. Now let's go to the next definition, which is climate adaptation. Now in human systems, the process of adjustment to actual or expected climate and its effects in order to moderate harm or exploit the beneficial opportunities is what is known as climate adaptation. How can we think about this definition through this? You know, through this entire experience that we are already going through. Let's look at the dinosaurs. How did they become extinct? When the entire climate started changing, right. That is how different kinds of species on this planet have adapted and have either become extinct or they have survived. So, remember Charles Darwin, Theory of the survival of the fittest. You must think about adaptation in that context. When we really know whether our body, the food that is going to land up on our table, is going to actually survive this climate heat. Instances that we are going through, recent research has said that in this past year itself, we as a human race have been exposed to 45 to 50 extra days of prolonged heat spells. Now how as a human race we are going to, over a period of time, respond, react, and adapt to these prolonged 50 days of heat exposure, only time can tell. But what can we do in terms of adapting to this actual situation is we stay indoors. When there are prolonged heat spells, we hydrate, we make sure we are in cooler environments, right. So these are ways in which we adapt. So when we think about also the aspect of exploiting beneficial opportunities out of this, from the SMEs point of view, we can think of this example of Millets. Millets is what we call as rag. In other you know, or in the local language or in the vernacular language, but India's Millet Mission has been a mission to empower farmers and tell them that no matter how harsh the weather gets or how scarce water becomes, we can still grow certain crops and you can survive and thrive in these extreme conditions. So exploiting beneficial opportunities means that we equip our agricultural sector, our SME sector and every section of the economy or the society, and tell them how they can exploit these opportunities that come with extreme heat or extreme rains and still thrive by finding a business opportunity in such a situation. Another example that I'll be giving you is that of the wiper blades on cars. When extreme heat events are while they are a part of reality, in some countries, like in the United States, they could come in the form of hurricanes and tornadoes causing a lot of rainfall at one period of time. Even as I speak and record the scores in the Himalayas itself, we are experiencing cloud bursts because of climate change, which causes extreme rains. And the destruction of several villages as landslides and avalanches bring down a lot of property, farming land, and even people, animals, and a lot of things down because of the gush of the rivers from those glaciers, right. So in such situations, how can one think about opportunities so that one can quickly adapt to this? So another example here that I can give you is, that of flood monitors, how you know, you could develop flood monitors, you could keep them by the river banks. You could use alert systems to send out sirens across the valley to alert villagers that there is of surge of rainwater coming down from the hills. So to move to higher places or to evacuate the area quickly, to use technology, to use satellite imagery, mapping real time data to manage disasters such as these. And also opportunities for rescue teams to reach the you know, sites very quickly. So, wiper blades, if, the wiper blades are old or they are not capable of you know, quickly wiping the windshield of the vehicle while the deluge of rain is so harsh, then it could cause a barrier or it could really you know, cause delays in rescue teams reaching the spots when there are such climate extreme events. So these are certain components that you could think about. One is the millets example for climate resilient and adaptable crops. One is the example of the wiper blades for cars and other you know, vehicles so that in extreme weather events they can still reach you know, emergency situations in on time. And another is using data and using satellite imagery and scientific evidence to predict the onset of extreme climate events. So this is how you could exploit beneficial opportunities through climate adaptation in these times. The third example or the third definition, which is very important today, is climate resilience. And what is climate resilience? Climate resilience is the capacity of social, economic, and environmental systems to cope with a hazardous event or a trend or a disturb disturbance, responding to it and reorganizing in ways that maintain the essential function, identity and structure, while also maintaining the capacity for adaptation, learning, and transformation. Now, a good example for that, you will also find in the book about how the local communities or the forest communities that live close to the forest or are very, very you know, capable of realizing what are the indicators that nature gives out when there is going to be a climate extreme event. You'll find a lot of examples mentioned here. So try to think about ways in which you could find opportunities for building climate resilience in communities. Because when a climate extreme event hits a community, it affects them in different ways. It causes societal imbalance, it causes a break in the food chain. It causes disruptions to portable water supply. They could be stranded in a place without water for several weeks or without food for several weeks. There could be vulnerable groups there, like women who may be pregnant, who may be having young babies, even older people who are not mobile. They cannot walk around. So for these vulnerable communities, it could affect them in many. Also, you might have read examples of tribes stuck in forests who do not interact with other groups because their immune systems are very low. If they come in contact with other populations, they could face diseases because they haven't been vaccinated, or they have you know, their immune systems are very different because they always live within the forest. So when extreme events hit such communities they have to display climate resilience, right. Because they need to not only respond to the situation, they also need to reorganize in ways that can maintain their essential functionality. They can't become cannibals, they cannot, you know, you eat each other up because there is scarcity of food. So you, one needs to think about maintaining a social norms. Structures and not letting people to turn into criminals. You know that a neighbor might go into another neighbor's house and steal the food if they know they have more food. So we need equilibrium to be maintained when such climate extreme events hit. And therefore, climate resilience is about this. It's about maintaining that capacity for adaptation to learn from communities who show resilience and transformation. A good example of how to learn from communities is the indigenous people or the tribal people who live in close contact with nature. There are examples of such indigenous communities in the Pacific Islands, in our own Western Ghats, in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands where they show climate resilience, where the leaders of the communities alert the community that there is a tsunami going to hit, or there is an extreme climate event going to occur. So this is how you should start saving up on food. This is how you should think about staying indoors. For a longer period of time, and this is how as a community we will get through this extreme event and still survive and thrive. So we have a lot of examples to look for in terms of climate resilience and in this recent G 20 summit that India showed leadership in, there were a lot of examples of this indigenous knowledge systems or traditional knowledge systems that they should be used and documented because we need to learn from what they have been doing because they live in such close contact with nature. Another example or another terminology that I want to kind of speak about here before we get into the module one is circular economy. Circular economy is defined as a current sustainable economic model in which products and materials are designed in such a way that, that they can be reused, remanufactured, recycled, or recovered. Now, throughout these modules, I'll give you a lot of examples about circular economy and SMEs have a wonderful way of being in the circular economy you know, discussion or in that space, because the bigger conglomerates are dependent on SMEs for this aspect of reusing, remanufacturing, recycling, and recovering materials. Another example or another terminology that I have spoken about earlier in terms of climate resilience, but I want to talk about it again, is indigenous knowledge. Indigenous knowledge, as I have said earlier, refers to the philosophies, including understanding the values, the skills, and the competencies needed that are developed, but, and also experienced by several societies that have had a long history of interaction with their natural surroundings. So when you look at my book, Media and Climate Change, and you go to the page of the chapter 6, page number 71. You will see this chapter called Creating Communities to Report on Climate Change. And there you will see a case study of how several indigenous communities have showed an understanding of climate change. I would particularly like you to look at page 77 and table 6.1, which has an overview of community responses to climate and socioeconomic change. Where we asked, when we conducted this res research in the western cuts of the south southern region of India, which is essentially India's rain forest because they are home to a lot of all flora and fauna, but also to 50 million human beings. We ask these indigenous communities on how did they think about these prolonged heat spells. And they talked about it in terms of sudden cloudbursts or completely long heat spells that the summer was no longer now two months, but it sometimes extended to four months. There was just no sign of rain. You remember the movie Lagaan, right. Where you know, these villagers in, a remote or a arid place like Kachchh were waiting for rain for several, several months and rain was nowhere in sight and they were levied heavy taxes, but they were not able to pay those taxes because they were not able to grow any crops on their fields. So you saw through that example of the movie Lagaan, how the entire community gets affected, how the entire village was affected by it. So this is how climate change and climate extreme events can cause a lot of disruptions, and this is where indigenous knowledge can be utilized to help the communities to respond to such changes, how can they show their response. They can show it by bringing in a variety of crops, crop rotation, not depending on the rains completely. Like I gave you the example of millets and how it affects labor migration, how it causes them to skip meals, how they end up selling their assets, borrowing money, all these are the responses of the community when they end up in a situation of prolonged heat spills or sudden cloudbursts and, these are ways in which SMEs can also find ideas to collaborate like I gave you the example of flood monitors or weather indicators that you can develop as Apps to help these farming communities or you can give them examples of how to grow certain crops and what those crops, how they can think about you know, expanding the value chain for those crops and making sure they know how to store those crops? And you know, that extreme weather events, again, do not damage the storage of the crops in the godown. How to find a market for those you know, goods? All these come under the SMEs you know, gamut of functioning. And I hope you can think about several examples while I have been talking about this so that you can leverage these in terms of the you know, the help or the interventions that you propose for such communities that are affected because of climate change or extreme weather events.
